title: Build Anything with Tmux, Here's How
summary: A video tutorial on using Tmux, a terminal multiplexer, for development setup and configuration. The content covers installation and practical usage of Tmux for building applications.
writeup: A hands-on tutorial positioning tmux as foundational infrastructure for agentic engineering: the argument is that persistent, detachable terminal sessions on a remote VPS are what separate serious agent operators from developers who run everything locally and lose work when their laptop sleeps. The video walks from a local tmux install through SSH access to a Hostinger VPS, covering the session/window/pane hierarchy and the key shortcuts for splitting and navigating panes. The through-line is that tmux lets multiple agents run concurrently for hours or days, detached from any single device, matching how the video describes top AI developers operating today.
key_points: tmux sessions persist independently of the client connection, so an agent running inside a tmux session continues uninterrupted whether the controlling laptop dies, goes to sleep, or is replaced by a phone SSH client via an app like Terminus.

The session/window/pane hierarchy is the structural core: a session is the whole workspace, windows are browser-like tabs within it, and panes are split-screen terminals within a window, all addressable programmatically so one agent can read what another agent is doing in a separate pane.

The practical VPS recommendation is a Hostinger KVM2 plan running Ubuntu Linux with tmux 3.4 pre-installed; the workflow is to SSH in from any device, start a named tmux session with `tmux new-s <name>`, and detach with Ctrl-B then D, leaving all agents running on the server.

Enabling mouse support immediately after install (`echo 'set -g mouse on'` appended to `.tmux.conf`) is described as non-negotiable, as it allows clicking between panes rather than relying solely on keyboard shortcuts.

The primary pane-split shortcuts are Ctrl-B then `%` for a horizontal split and Ctrl-B then `"` for a vertical split, with each resulting pane capable of hosting a separate agent process such as Claude Code or Codex.

The shift from local development to VPS-hosted tmux sessions reflects a broader change in agent workload durations: the video notes that a year prior the longest agent run was 5 to 10 minutes, whereas features like the `/go` command in Codex and Gemini now enable multi-day autonomous runs.
